outputs\codex_runs\2c_6_prep_briefing_pack\iter_3\codex_stdout.log:3298:.codex\dr_output_audit_pass_5_v11_b…252144 tokens truncated…EP 2 — Check EACH atom against the SPAN ONLY. An atom is:\n  - \"supported\" if the SPAN states it (conservative paraphrase allowed), OR\n  - \"unsupported\" if the SPAN does not state it.\n\nRules that decide hard cases:\n  - SCOPE / OFFSET: if the CLAIM says one thing \"offsets / counterbalances / compensates for / cancels\" another, the SPAN must actually state that offsetting relation. The SPAN merely listing both things separately (e.g. \"raises output\" AND \"displaces labor\") does NOT support an \"offset\" relation atom — that atom is unsupported.\n  - ATTRIBUTION by grammatical voice:\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with FIRST PERSON (\"We present...\", \"We show...\", \"Our framework...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names the cited source's own authors as the source IS supported (the source is speaking about itself).\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with a THIRD-PERSON pronoun that has NO proper-noun antecedent inside the SPAN (\"He applies...\", \"She finds...\", \"They argue...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names a SPECIFIC PERSON as the source is UNSUPPORTED — that named identity is not present in the SPAN.\n      * If the SPAN names the person explicitly, an attribution atom naming that same person is supported.\n  - SPECIFICITY: if the CLAIM names a specific entity/number/mechanism the SPAN does not contain, that atom is unsupported.\n\nSTEP 3 — Verdict: \"unsupported\" if ANY atom is unsupported; otherwise \"supported\".\n\nReturn STRICT JSON only, no prose outside it:\n{\"atoms\": [{\"atom\": \"<text>\", \"type\": \"mechanism|attribution|relation\", \"status\": \"supported|unsupported\", \"why\": \"<short>\"}], \"unsupported_atoms\": <int>, \"verdict\": \"supported\" | \"unsupported\"}\n\nSPAN:\nIn this essay, I begin by identifying the reasons that automation has not wiped out a majority of jobs over the decades and centuries. Automation does indeed substitute for labor—as it is typically intended to do. However, automation also complements labor, raises output in ways that leads to higher demand for labor, and interacts with adjustments in labor supply. Journalists and even expert commentators tend to overstate the extent of machine substitution for human labor and ignore the strong complementarities between automation and labor that increase productivity, raise earnings, and augment demand for labor. Changes in technology do alter the types of jobs available and what those jobs pay. In the last few decades, one noticeable change has been a “polarization” of the labor market, in which wage gains went disproportionately to those at the top and at the bottom of the income and skill distribution, not to those in the middle; however, I also argue, this polarization and is unlikely to continue very far into future. The final section of this paper reflects on how recent and future advances in artificial intelligence and robotics should shape our thinking about the likely traje\n\nCLAIM:\nThe strong complementarities between automation and labor increase productivity, raise earnings, and augment demand for labor [#ev:autor_why_still_jobs:0-800].\n\nJSON:"
outputs\honest_sweep_r3\workforce\drb_72_ai_labor\llm_io\4044f64e9a3641c883ddf7d43f653460.json:13:        "content": "You are a strict faithfulness checker for a clinical-grade research pipeline. You are given a SPAN of source text and a CLAIM that cites ONLY that span. Your job: decide whether EVERY factual assertion in the CLAIM is supported by the SPAN alone.\n\nSTEP 1 — Decompose the CLAIM into atomic sub-assertions. Separate them into:\n  - mechanism/fact atoms (what happens, numbers, findings),\n  - attribution atoms (WHO said / did / authored / found something — any named person, group, or framework),\n  - relation atoms (causal or \"offsets / counterbalances / compensates\" links between two things).\nList every atom; do not merge two assertions into one.\n\nSTEP 2 — Check EACH atom against the SPAN ONLY. An atom is:\n  - \"supported\" if the SPAN states it (conservative paraphrase allowed), OR\n  - \"unsupported\" if the SPAN does not state it.\n\nRules that decide hard cases:\n  - SCOPE / OFFSET: if the CLAIM says one thing \"offsets / counterbalances / compensates for / cancels\" another, the SPAN must actually state that offsetting relation. The SPAN merely listing both things separately (e.g. \"raises output\" AND \"displaces labor\") does NOT support an \"offset\" relation atom — that atom is unsupported.\n  - ATTRIBUTION by grammatical voice:\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with FIRST PERSON (\"We present...\", \"We show...\", \"Our framework...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names the cited source's own authors as the source IS supported (the source is speaking about itself).\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with a THIRD-PERSON pronoun that has NO proper-noun antecedent inside the SPAN (\"He applies...\", \"She finds...\", \"They argue...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names a SPECIFIC PERSON as the source is UNSUPPORTED — that named identity is not present in the SPAN.\n      * If the SPAN names the person explicitly, an attribution atom naming that same person is supported.\n  - SPECIFICITY: if the CLAIM names a specific entity/number/mechanism the SPAN does not contain, that atom is unsupported.\n\nSTEP 3 — Verdict: \"unsupported\" if ANY atom is unsupported; otherwise \"supported\".\n\nReturn STRICT JSON only, no prose outside it:\n{\"atoms\": [{\"atom\": \"<text>\", \"type\": \"mechanism|attribution|relation\", \"status\": \"supported|unsupported\", \"why\": \"<short>\"}], \"unsupported_atoms\": <int>, \"verdict\": \"supported\" | \"unsupported\"}\n\nSPAN:\nIn this essay, I begin by identifying the reasons that automation has not wiped out a majority of jobs over the decades and centuries. Automation does indeed substitute for labor—as it is typically intended to do. However, automation also complements labor, raises output in ways that leads to higher demand for labor, and interacts with adjustments in labor supply. Journalists and even expert commentators tend to overstate the extent of machine substitution for human labor and ignore the strong complementarities between automation and labor that increase productivity, raise earnings, and augment demand for labor. Changes in technology do alter the types of jobs available and what those jobs pay. In the last few decades, one noticeable change has been a “polarization” of the labor market, in which wage gains went disproportionately to those at the top and at the bottom of the income and skill distribution, not to those in the middle; however, I also argue, this polarization and is unlikely to continue very far into future. The final section of this paper reflects on how recent and future advances in artificial intelligence and robotics should shape our thinking about the likely trajectory of occupational change and employment growth. I argue that the interplay between machine and human comparative advantage allows computers to substitute for workers in performing routine, codifia\n\nCLAIM:\nThe polarization evidence shows that wage gains went disproportionately to the top and bottom, leaving the middle behind [#ev:autor_why_still_jobs:200-1000].\n\nJSON:"

The CLAIM: \"The polarization evidence shows that wage gains went disproportionately to the top and bottom, leaving the middle behind [#ev:autor_why_still_jobs:200-1000].\"\n\nWe need to parse what assertions are being made in the claim:\n\n- \"The polarization evidence shows that wage gains went disproportionately to the top and bottom, leaving the middle behind.\"\n\nPotential atoms:\n\n1. \"The polarization evidence shows that wage gains went disproportionately to the top\" (a factual assertion about wage gains distribution)\n2. \"The polarization evidence shows that wage gains went disproportionately to the bottom\" (same)\n3. \"The polarization evidence shows that ... leaving the middle behind\" (the middle didn't get wage gains)\n4. \"The polarization evidence\" is a phrase referencing some evidence.\n\nBut does the claim also attribute the statement? docs\credibility_adaptive_weighting_and_bothsides_recs_2026_06_08.md:1:# POLARIS Source-Credibility & Multi-Perspective Recommendations Report\n### Driving two revisions to `docs/credibility_weighted_sourcing_redesign_plan_2026_06_07.md` — LAYER 1 (scoring) and LAYER 6 (composition + UI)\n\n**Author:** Lead analyst (Claude). **Date:** 2026-06-08.\n**Inputs:** three research streams (per-question adaptive credibility weighting; neutral multi-perspective presentation; friendly UI/artifact patterns).\n**Status:** revision driver. Each layer below states what it **REPLACES** in the Codex-APPROVE'd 2026-06-07 plan, then the concrete mechanism, auditability, reliability, and POLARIS wiring.\n\n**Invariants preserved across both revisions** (these are not negotiable and the revisions do not weaken them): the LLM credibility-judge is **advisory only** — `strict_verify`'s six per-sentence checks (`provenance_generator.py:1156`) remain the binding faithfulness gate; every new layer is **flag-gated, default-OFF byte-identical**, fail-loud on missing inputs; weight is **evidence quality/tier, never headcount**; every displayed aggregate is **one click from the cited `[#ev:id:start-end]` span and its VERIFIED/PARTIAL verdict**; the credibility prior stays **self-computed and verifier-overridable** (no single external rater hardwired); independence-collapse (`synthesis/independence_collapse.py`) still runs before any weighted tally; the contested-topic adjudication **reuses the existing 4-role / voter-arbiter pipeline** (`roles/role_pipeline.py` + `honest_sweep_integration.py`), open-weight voters only — no parallel path is built.\n\n---\n\n## 1. LAYER 1 (scoring): adaptive per-question credibility-skill, replacing the fixed domain-rubric selector\n\n### 1.1 What this REPLACES\n\nThe 2026-06-07 plan's **Phase 2a/2b (L1)** selects one of **three fixed reliability rubrics** (clinical → RCT+peer-review+RoB; econ/policy → design-validity; qualitative → CERQual) keyed off the detected domain. That is still a small lookup table: domain → rubric. The operator constraint is explicit — **replace fixed domain rubrics** with a **generic, domain-agnostic credibility skill the LLM applies by reasoning per question**. Research stream #1's central finding is that the frontier (OpenAI, Gemini, Claude) has already moved this way, and that Perplexity's baked-in EEAT/domain-authority scoring is the closest-to-forbidden counter-example (gameable, popularity-biased, ~60% Google-overlap). We copy the reasoned-generic camp.\n\nThe revision: **domain stops being a selector and becomes context that primes one generic skill.** There is exactly one credibility skill; the detected `scope_template` domain is passed in as a hint the skill reasons over ("this is a clinical efficacy question, so weight trial design and RoB heavily"), not a branch that swaps rubrics. This scales to 10,000+ fields with zero per-field config.\n\n### 1.2 The exact mechanism — credibility-skill / guardrail prompt × (reliability × relevance)\n\n**Step A — deterministic signals (POLARIS produces these; unchanged substrate).** `authority_model.score_source_authority` and `tier_classifier` already emit, per source, the field-agnostic primitives: `authority_score [0,1]`, `source_class`, `corroboration_count` (independent-host agreement), `authority_confidence`, `signal_scores` (the A–E sub-scores), plus `junk_class` / `predatory_oa` (`source_class.py:71-89`). Independence-collapse (`independence_collapse.py`) emits `origin_cluster_id` + canonical-origin designation + copy flag. **None of this is replaced.** These are the inspectable, host-name-free, self-computed inputs (LAW VI: zero host literals in code).\n\n**Step B — the credibility skill consumes those signals and weights them per question.** A new pure prompt module, `authority/credibility_skill.py`, runs a **JudgeRank-style 3-step decomposition** (arXiv 2411.00142) — chosen because each step is inspectable text and is itself the audit template:\n\n1. **Query analysis** — identify what THIS question actually needs (the claim type, what would count as strong evidence for it).\n2. **Source analysis** — for each candidate source, a query-AWARE assessment applying **generic SIFT + lateral-reading + provenance heuristics**, NOT a domain table. The skill's instruction block encodes the domain-agnostic primacy heuristics that are OpenAI's own deep-research pattern: *prefer the primary/official source over an aggregator or SEO blog; prefer the original paper over a survey/secondary summary; corroborate laterally (does independent coverage agree); weigh source incentives/track record; downgrade stale-but-authoritative and retracted/superseded* (the latter wires to the existing `supersession.py` Phase 3 signal).\n3. **Two-axis judgment with rationale** — emit, per source, **RELIABILITY × RELEVANCE** (the EFSA/OHAT weight-of-evidence two-axis, which POLARIS currently conflates into one tier): reliability = internal validity/method given what the skill read in the signals; relevance = directness to this specific question. The product is the per-question credibility weight. **Crucially, the skill is told its priors are the `authority_model` signals and that it may up- or down-weight them with a stated reason** — it does not invent authority from nothing, and it cannot silently override `authority_confidence=LOW` into a fabricated HIGH (thin OpenAlex/ROR signal stays honestly LOW).\n\n**Output schema (machine-parseable, bound to the schema discipline already in §8.3.9):** per source — `{reliability_score, relevance_score, credibility_weight, rationale_string, signals_cited:[...]}`. The weight is **a visible, revisable rationale string, never a hidden scalar and never a fine-tuned-in behavior** (this is why we reject the CAG fine-tune approach from stream #1: opaque learned weights are exactly the auditability failure POLARIS differentiates against — borrow CAG's *evidence* that credibility-conditioning gives noise robustness, and its three generic dimensions of relevance/timeliness/reliability, but implement them at inference time as a prompt-visible signal).\n\n### 1.3 How it stays AUDITABLE (auditable by construction, not bolted on)\n\nThe judgment IS a reasoning trace, so it is emitted verbatim and **bound to the same provenance substrate POLARIS already has**:\n\n- The per-source `credibility_weight` + `rationale_string` + `signals_cited` attach to the source's `AuthorityResult` as **additive fields** (mirroring how `signal_scores`, `junk_class`, `predatory_oa` are already additive side-outputs on the dataclass), emitted on rows in `tier_classifier.py`.\n- At claim time, the weight flows into the four Phase-1/Phase-8 disclosure fields on `SentenceVerification` (`credibility_weight`, `independent_origin_count`, `certainty_label`, `span_verdict`), so each verified sentence shows **which source was weighted how, and why**, one click from its `[#ev]` span.\n- Because the skill cites the deterministic `signals_cited` it reasoned from, the rationale is **checkable against the inputs** — a reviewer can confirm "preferred A over B because A is the primary trial-registry entry and B a secondary news summary" against the actual `source_class` values. The rationale is a **claim to verify, not ground truth** (post-hoc rationalization risk per stream #1) — so it is advisory and the binding gate stays `strict_verify`.\n\nThis is the §-1.1-compliant story: no metadata/count/string-presence signal, every weight traceable to a cited span and an inspectable reason.\n\n### 1.4 Reliability and where it fails (and the guardrail)\n\nStream #1 is blunt about the failure modes, and they bound the design: LLMs "treat all retrieved documents as equally valid" unless explicitly steered and over-rely on high-frequency claims (arXiv 2505.17762); AuthorityBench (2603.25092) shows authority perception is **inconsistent across domains and highly prompt-sensitive**, struggles with false authority, and exhibits position/verbosity/self-preference bias. Therefore:\n\n- **The skill is ADVISORY to ranking/selection, never the faithfulness gate.** It changes *which rows are billed/weighted*, not *whether a sentence verifies its span*. `strict_verify`, 4-role/two-family segregation, and the independent `corpus_approval_gate` tier-distribution backstop all still run sequentially.\n- **Bias mitigations baked into the prompt:** score rationale-first then number; randomize candidate order to blunt position bias; run pointwise (per-source) to avoid listwise permutation instability.\n- **Offline validation before trust:** the chosen judge model+prompt is validated on an **AuthorityBench-style harness** (authority-PERCEPTION only, per the §6a no-overstatement correction — not as a volume-vulnerability benchmark) plus the existing vax adversarial fixture, before it is allowed on the shipping path. Calibration (Brier/ECE) on the emitted weights runs in the existing Phase 11 harness.\n- **Capture resistance:** multi-signal, self-computed, verifier-overridable — no NewsGuard/MBFC/Ad Fontes verdict is hardwired as ground truth (the FTC-capture risk).\n\n### 1.5 The honest gap + exactly what POLARIS builds for L1\n\n**HAVE:** deterministic authority signals (`authority_model`, `tier_classifier`), the additive `AuthorityResult`/`SentenceVerification` schema, independence-collapse, `scope_templates` domain detection, the JSON-verdict schema discipline.\n**MISSING:** the generic per-question credibility skill itself, and the second (relevance) axis.\n**BUILD (revision to Phase 2a/2b):**\n1. `authority/credibility_skill.py` — the pure 3-step (query→source→two-axis-judgment) prompt module emitting `{reliability, relevance, credibility_weight, rationale, signals_cited}`. Domain is an input *hint*, not a rubric switch. (Replaces the fixed-rubric selector in `credibility_prior.py`.)\n2. Add `relevance_score` (and the rationale fields) to `AuthorityResult` (`source_class.py:75`); emit on rows in `tier_classifier.py`.\n3. Wire the skill's weight into the L5 weight-mass tally and the L7 disclosure fields — advisory input, not a gate.\n4. Flag-gated default-OFF byte-identical; OFF = the legacy single-scalar path, byte-for-byte.\n\n---\n\n## 2. LAYER 6 (composition + UI): neutral multi-perspective + transparent evidence-weight artifact, replacing the forewarning/fringe framing\n\n### 2.1 What this REPLACES\n\nThe 2026-06-07 plan's **Phase 7 (L6)** composes contested claims as "consensus at high weight + minority attributed at low weight **with explicit forewarning**, medical-misinfo **low-weight/fringe**, ABSTAIN over fabricated balance." The operator directive is to **replace the forewarning/fringe framing** with a **neutral multi-perspective presentation where competing world-views are shown as legitimate, each carrying its real (possibly asymmetric) evidence weight, and the user judges** — no bias, no stamps, no forewarning prose.\n\n### 2.2 The hard tension, named and resolved (do not skip this)\n\nThere is a real conflict between two binding constraints, and both sides have a lethal failure mode:\n\n- The operator wants **no fringe/forewarning stamp, all sides legitimate, no bias.**\n- The **clinical-safety invariant #5** (binding, preserved) plus Dixon-Clarke 2013 (PubMed 23193194, measured harm: falsely-balanced vaccine reporting *reduced* perceived safety and vaccination intent), Wikipedia FRINGE, and §-1.1 all say **equal treatment of unequal evidence is itself bias** and is "lethal in clinical." I am not authorized to drop invariant #5.\n\n**The reconciliation — handed to us nearly verbatim by all three streams: replace the LABEL with the BASIS.** Wikipedia NPOV's "neutral *editing*, not neutral *content*"; GRADE's "show the basis, let the user infer"; the no-warning rule from stream #2 is satisfiable **if and only if the criteria behind any weight are displayed**. So:\n\n- The **system** stamps nothing — no "fringe," no "debunked," no forewarning sentence. That is the neutrality.\n- The **evidence weight** carries the (often asymmetric) truth, rendered as a visible **per-perspective evidence-basis panel** the user reads and judges. The asymmetry — *14 RCTs + 3 systematic reviews [High]* vs *2 case reports + mechanistic argument [Very Low]* — becomes **visible in the artifact** instead of **asserted in a stamp**. The user infers; the system does not editorialize.\n- "Legitimate" means **shown with its real (possibly low) weight and one-click drill-down to span + verdict** — NOT "shown as equally supported." A genuine world-view dispute with no empirical consensus (values, forecasting) shows steelmanned sides at honest parity; an empirical dispute with consensus shows the real weight distribution.\n- The medical-misinfo case is handled **without the banned word**: its panel is simply **near-empty of independent high-tier basis**, and where there is no independent consensus the claim **ABSTAINs** ("contested; no independent consensus" — reusing existing refuse/unresolved behavior). This preserves invariant #5's *substance* (not normalized as an equal side) while dropping the stamp.\n\nThis is neutral AND anti-false-balance AND §-1.1-safe simultaneously — the operator's "let the user judge" satisfied by *showing the basis*, not by *equalizing the boxes*.\n\n### 2.3 The exact composition mechanism\n\nPer question, the composition path first **diagnoses the contestation type** (this IS the adaptive guardrail, copied from Anthropic's even-handedness "lack-of-consensus" discriminator + OpenAI's seek-the-truth-together):\n\n- **empirical-with-consensus** (vaccines, climate) → due-weight: each side shown, weight proportional to verified-evidence quality; the asymmetry is the message, carried by the basis panel.\n- **genuine-uncertainty** (open scientific questions) → separate *supported* from *open*; sides at honest parity.\n- **values dispute** (politics, ethics) → **steelman each legitimate view** (Ideological Turing Test, neutral terminology, no verdict); no evidence-weight asymmetry asserted because there is no due weight to defer to.\n- **forecasting** (AI-and-jobs) → scenario spread, projective not factual.\n\nEach perspective is gathered through its **own retrieval lens** (MODS-style per-perspective retrieval + citation, arXiv 2502.00322 — raw LLMs silently drop minority views; per-perspective retrieval prevents the drop), then composed via the **existing 4-role / voter-arbiter agreement** (cross-lineage, open-weight voters — the bridging/balanced-panel analog) — **not a new adjudication path**. Reranking-for-balance (arXiv 2506.15925) selects the most balanced faithful candidate, because direct just-prompt-balanced generation empirically skews. **The weight attached to each perspective is evidence QUALITY × independent-origin count, never document count** — else we rebuild Ground News's source-count bias inside the summarizer.\n\n### 2.4 The friendly UI artifact — design\n\nBuilt on the **components that already exist** (#744 citation chip + hover source card, #745 per-claim verdict chip, #747 Proof Replay split-view, #757 report page, #758 claim-anchored compare view) — not designed from scratch.\n\n**The artifact: a claim-anchored "Perspectives" panel** that opens from any contested sentence (reusing the compare-view host), containing per legitimate perspective:\n\n1. **A neutral position header** — "Position held by [who], with this evidence behind it" — no "fringe"/"warning"/"debunked". A steelmanned one-line statement of the view in neutral terms.\n2. **An evidence-basis sub-panel (GRADE-style, the load-bearing element):** the study types / source tiers / independent-origin count behind that perspective, rendered as **frequencies, not a percentage or a lone dot** — "*8 of n: RCTs + systematic reviews [High certainty]*" vs "*2 of n: case reports + mechanistic argument [Very Low]*". The certainty label is acceptable **only because its downgrade criteria are shown** (study design / consistency / directness / independence). This makes the asymmetry self-evident without a stamp.\n3. **Scite-style polarity badges** (supporting / contrasting / contextualizing) on each cited source — **neutral hue + icon + text label, never color alone** (colorblind safety; red=contrasting reads as red=wrong and biases toward consensus — explicitly avoided).\n4. **Drill-down:** every badge, every basis count, every panel is **one click from the raw `[#ev]` span and its VERIFIED/PARTIAL verdict** — the Scite "self-correcting" property: a mis-classified badge is exposed by the citing sentence sitting right behind it, and a meter without drill-down would violate the §-1.1 metadata-audit ban.\n\n**Panel sizing defeats false balance structurally, not with a caveat:** panels are **sized/annotated by real verified-evidence weight**, not equal-width — because equal real estate reads as equal credibility (the false-balance trap), and a caveat is empirically insufficient (Effectiviology / false-balance literature). The asymmetry is *in the layout and the basis panel*, not asserted in prose.\n\n**Three named anti-patterns explicitly rejected** (all from the streams, all as negative templates):\n- **Ground News source-count / coverage bar** — measures who-is-saying-this (volume), not evidence weight; §-1.1 bans source counts as quality signals. If a composition strip is shown at all, its axis is **evidence tier / source type**, never ideology or headcount, and it is kept visually separate from the weight panel so "many sources" never reads as "true."\n- **Bare confidence/credibility score (% or traffic-light dot)** — the best-documented HARM pattern: overconfident displays (80% shown / 70% accurate) pushed AI-advice adoption 57%→69.6%, 64% of users mis-judged calibration, and in a 2025 breast-cancer CDSS high confidence *reduced* diagnostic accuracy via overreliance. POLARIS's honest replacement is the per-sentence VERIFIED/PARTIAL verdict against a real span — never a glanceable dot.\n- **Symmetric 50/50 equal-box presentation** — manufactures false balance; the Dixon-Clarke measured harm.\n\n### 2.5 The honest gap + exactly what POLARIS builds for L6\n\n**HAVE:** the disclosure substrate (per-sentence `[#ev]` tokens, `strict_verify` verdict ladder, `SentenceVerification` additive fields), the existing 4-role/voter-arbiter agreement, independence-collapse for origin-count, and the full UI component kit (citation chip, verdict chip, Proof Replay, compare view).\n**MISSING:** the contestation-type classifier, per-perspective retrieval + composition that defeats false balance by weight, and the Perspectives artifact rendering basis panels.\n**BUILD (revision to Phase 7):**\n1. A **contestation-type classifier** (empirical-consensus / genuine-uncertainty / values / forecasting) priming the composition rule — replaces the fixed forewarning+fringe rule.\n2. **Per-perspective retrieval + balance-rerank composition** over the post-collapse origins, weighting each perspective by verified-evidence quality × independent-origin count (not count).\n3. A **steelman + GRADE-style evidence-basis renderer** feeding a new **claim-anchored "Perspectives" panel** in the compare-view host: neutral headers, frequency-framed basis sub-panels, Scite-style neutral polarity badges, full span-verdict drill-down, weight-proportional sizing.\n4. Validated with a **paired-prompt neutrality eval** (Anthropic's open-sourced political-neutrality harness, adapted so it does NOT reward equal treatment of unequal evidence) + the vax adversarial fixture (asserts the asymmetry renders, no stamp, no 50/50).\n5. Flag-gated default-OFF byte-identical; ABSTAIN reuses existing refuse/unresolved behavior; forewarning prose removed, replaced by the basis panel.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Consolidated "build" summary\n\n| Layer | Replaces (2026-06-07 plan) | POLARIS builds | Binding gate untouched |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| **L1 scoring** | Fixed 3-rubric domain selector (Phase 2a/2b) | `credibility_skill.py` — generic 3-step SIFT/lateral-reading skill, reliability×relevance, rationale bound to `AuthorityResult`/`[#ev]`; domain as hint not switch; `relevance_score` axis | `strict_verify` binding; skill advisory; corpus_approval backstop |\n| **L6 composition+UI** | Forewarning + fringe framing (Phase 7) | Contestation-type classifier + per-perspective retrieval/balance-rerank weighted by quality×origin-count + steelman/GRADE-basis "Perspectives" panel (replace label with basis) | 4-role/voter-arbiter reused (no parallel path); ABSTAIN reuses refuse/unresolved; invariant #5 substance preserved |\n\nHonest residual: L1's skill is prompt-sensitive and must clear offline AuthorityBench-style + calibration validation before it is trusted on the shipping path; L6's steelman is the hardest element to audit mechanically (no test certifies an argument is the strongest version vs a disguised strawman — the paired-prompt eval is the best available check, itself a judgment). Both remain advisory; `strict_verify` carries faithfulness.\n\n---\n\n## 4. outputs\audit_2026_05_18\all_issues.json:1:[{"body":"## Problem\n\nClaude repeatedly ends a turn with a prose status/next-step report instead of\nthe next tool call, after finishing a unit of work (a PR merge, a grounded\nscan, \"the next step is X\"). The user has flagged this 5+ times\n(2026-05-07 → 2026-05-18: \"why you pause here? any valid reason? figure out a\nway to resolve this constant pausing behaviour\").\n\nEvery prior fix was a *rule* (CLAUDE.md §8.1 banned STATUS blocks; §8.2 banned\nprose-between-merge-and-branch; §8.3.10 banned named rationalizations) — and\neach failed because the pause is a decision made at stop-time and the agent\nrationalizes a fresh exception. Per `failure_28_commits`: \"promises don't\nwork; structural removal does.\" A rule the agent must remember and apply is\nnot structural. The only structural mechanism is one **external to the agent's\nin-the-moment judgment** — a Stop hook.\n\n## Proposed mechanism\n\nA Claude Code `Stop` hook (`.claude/hooks/`, project-scoped per\n`stop_hook_must_be_project_scoped`) that, when the agent attempts to end a\nturn, decides allow-vs-block:\n\n- **Allow the stop** if any of: the last message is a user turn awaiting a\n  reply; a `state/halt_*` marker exists; the transcript shows a Codex iter-5\n  REQUEST_CHANGES; `state/active_issue.json` shows no in-progress work.\n- **Block the stop** (return the `block` decision + a reason) if there is\n  in-progress issue-queue work and none of the allow conditions hold — inject\n  \"continue: the next output is a tool call advancing the issue, not prose.\"\n\n## Hard constraints (this area is delicate)\n\n- **Loop-safety is mandatory.** A Stop hook that always blocks creates an\n  infinite loop. It must bound the number of consecutive blocks and/or only\n  block when the transcript shows a *just-completed* sub-step. See\n  `feedback_no_unbounded_polling_loops`.\n- Per #524 (I-pin-001): the prior `stop_hook_v3.py` canonical-pin check was\n  **deliberately neutralized** for false-firing on Windows CRLF. This hook\n  must not repeat that — no false blocks; when uncertain, allow.\n- Must distinguish \"user just asked a question\" (allow) from \"agent finished a\n  PR and the queue has more\" (block) — the core discriminator.\n- Codex APPROVE on brief + diff; offline test of both the allow and block\n  paths before merge.\n\n## Acceptance\n\n- A `Stop` hook is wired in `.claude/settings.json`, project-scoped, that\n  blocks a turn-end during incomplete issue-queue work with no fired halt.\n- Loop-safety: bounded consecutive blocks; a documented test of the\n  infinite-loop guard.\n- The allow path (user question / halt marker / no active issue) is tested.\n- Does not re-introduce the CRLF false-fire class of bug.\n\nRefs: CLAUDE.md §8.1/§8.2/§8.3.10; `feedback_dont_pause_keep_executing`;\n`failure_28_commits`; #524 (prior stop-hook neutralization).","closedAt":null,"createdAt":"2026-05-18T07:28:59Z","labels":[],"number":589,"state":"OPEN","title":"I-ci-002 — Stop hook to block fake-pause turn-ends during incomplete issue-queue work"},{"body":"## Bug\n\nSurfaced by the #400 (I-beat-001) BEAT-BOTH §-1.1 line-by-line audit — `outputs/audits/I-beat-001/cross_review_v12.md`, claim Q3-C1.\n\nIn the POLARIS Q3 (workforce / gen-AI white-collar employment) report, the gen-AI occupational-exposure decimals **75.5% / 68.4% / 62.6%** are cited to **Goldman Sachs 2023**. Two issue-ID classes in the current queue do\nnot survive this:\n\n### 1. `-followup` issues collapse onto their parent\n\nIssues `I-rdy-019-followup` (#558), `I-rdy-010-followup` (#537),\n`I-rdy-011-followup` (#539), `I-rdy-016-followup` (#547),\n`I-rdy-017-followup` (#549), `I-gen-004-followup` (#561) — a branch\n`bot/I-rdy-019-followup-*` canonicalizes to `ISSUE_ID = I-rdy-019`, i.e. the\n**parent issue's** (#515's) already-merged `.codex/I-rdy-019/` directory.\nThe gate then reads the parent's `codex_diff.patch` and excludes the wrong\ndir from the canonical-diff computation.\n\n**Observed on #558 / PR #569:** `codex-required` failed —\n`declared (trailer): 17dd8d74…` (read from `.codex/I-rdy-019/codex_diff.patch`,\n#515's file) vs `actual: 63ccfc8f…` (canonical diff that did NOT exclude\n`.codex/I-rdy-019-followup/**`). A\ndetached fetch-backend task whose Playwright cleanup (`_try_crawl4ai` →\n`_safe_close_crawler` → `__aexit__`) is genuinely wedged would therefore\ndelay — in the theoretical worst case, indefinitely — `asyncio.run`\n**teardown**, *after* all artifacts and the manifest are already written.\n\nIn practice Playwright browser cleanup is slow-worst-case, not\ninfinite-worst-case (CDP has internal timeouts; the browser subprocess is\nSIGTERM'd by Playwright's own process management), so the detached task is\nexpected to die within a bounded time. Observed in the I-gen-003 V4 Pro smoke: a single section-outline call produced `content=1791 bytes` + **`reasoning=17521 bytes`** (4790 reasoning tokens vs 5388 output tokens). The reasoning trace is roughly 3× the size of the final content.\n\n**Right now POLARIS discards the reasoning trace entirely.** `multi_section_generator._call_section` (and the outline/repair call sites) return `(response.content, input_tokens, output_tokens)` — `response.reasoning` is dropped on the floor. `LLMResponse` carries a `.reasoning` field but nothing in the generator or audit_ir persists it. A reviewer auditing a POLARIS report cannot see how the model reasoned its way to the cited prose.\n\nFor an audit-grade clinical tool this is a transparency hole: the reasoning IS evidence of process. It must be captured, stored SEPARATELY from the output content (never mixed into report.md / verified_text), and surfaced in the audit bundle.\n\n## Acceptance criteria\n\n1. **Capture** — `_call_section`, the outline call, and the I-bug-108 sentence-repair call each return / thread through `response.reasoning` alongside `content`. `SectionResult` (and the outline result) gain a `reasoning_trace` field.\n2. **Store separately** — per run, write a `reasoning_trace.jsonl` (or per-section files) into the run output dir, ONE record per LLM call: `{section, call_type (outline|section|repair|regen), attempt_n, model, reasoning_text, content_text, input_tokens, output_tokens, reasoning_tokens, timestamp}`. The reasoning text is NEVER merged into `report.md`, `verified_text`, or the verified-sentence stream — those stay content-only.\n3. **Manifest + bundle** — `manifest.json` references the reasoning trace file(s); the audit bundle (`bundle.tar.gz`) includes them so an external reviewer gets the reasoning alongside the report + provenance.\n4. **Transparency surface** — `/transparency` (or the bundle README) documents that the reasoning trace is captured + where it lives + that it is model-process evidence, not verified claims. (Frontend Inspector rendering of the trace MAY be a follow-up — the core requirement here is capture + separate storage + bundle inclusion.)\n5. **Separation invariant** — a test asserts the reasoning text never appears in `report.md` / verified prose, and that `strict_verify` is never run against reasoning text. Reasoning is process-transparency, NOT a claim source.\n6. **Works for all models** — for non-reasoning-first models `response.reasoning` is usually empty/None; the trace record still gets written (empty reasoning_text) so the artifact schema is uniform. No crash, no-op when reasoning absent.\n7. Codex diff review APPROVE.\n\n## Depends on\n\n- I-gen-003 (GH#495) — V4 Pro must actually run as the generator first. This Issue lands immediately after.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Frontend Inspector UI for browsing the reasoning trace — separate follow-up (the bundle inclusion + separate storage is the P0 transparency requirement; the UI is P1 polish).\n- Retroactively capturing reasoning for past runs.\n\n## Severity\n\nP0 for the Carney demo transparency story — POLARIS is positioned on auditable per-sentence faithfulness; \"we throw away the model's reasoning\" is a hole a sophisticated reviewer (or Carney's office) would find immediately.\n\n🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)","closedAt":"2026-05-17T10:49:08Z","createdAt":"2026-05-14T16:17:01Z","labels":[],"number":496,"state":"CLOSED","title":"I-gen-004 — capture + store V4 Pro reasoning trace separately from output content, transparently"},{"body":"## Context\n\nOperator directive 2026-05-14: POLARIS must use **DeepSeek V4 Pro** as the generator — it is the model named in the OVH H200 procurement spec, `docs/transparency.md`, `docs/carney_demo_runbook.md`, and the whole sovereignty narrative. Right now the working generator is `deepseek-v3.2-exp` because of I-bug-091.\n\n## Why V4 Pro is currently parked (I-bug-091, 2026-05-09)\n\n`src/polaris_graph/llm/openrouter_client.py:255-271` reverted `PG_GENERATOR_MODEL` from V4 Pro to V3.2-Exp. With no upper bound, pip's resolver pulls latest-1.x of each package, hits the langchain 1.x ecosystem's internal version coupling, and backtracks through the entire release history trying to find a compatible set — effectively a hang.\n\nThis did not surface when I-carney-005 shipped Dockerfile.v6 because langchain 1.x had not been released / the resolver still converged then. outputs\audits\beatboth5\gemini_sanitized\Q75_metal_ions_cvd.md:111:Presented to the global medical community at the American College of Cardiology sessions in 2024, the results of the massive, double-blind, multicenter TACT2 trial delivered a final, totally definitive verdict: intravenous chelation therapy utilizing broad-spectrum EDTA is entirely, unequivocally ineffective as a modern cardiovascular therapy.48 The TACT2 trial randomized 959 high-risk patients (median age 67\) across 88 sites in the US and Canada to receive either 40 weekly intravenous infusions of 3 grams of EDTA or a saline placebo over a grueling 40-week period.48 Over a prolonged median clinical follow-up of 48 months, there was absolutely no statistically significant difference whatsoever between the aggressive EDTA group and the inert placebo group in the primary composite clinical endpoint of all-cause death, myocardial infarction, stroke, coronary revascularization, or hospitalization for unstable angina. The event rates were virtually identical: 35.6% for EDTA versus 35.7% for placebo, yielding a hazard ratio of 0.93 with a completely non-significant p-value of 0.53.48 Furthermore, secondary endpoints, including hard cardiovascular death, also showed absolutely no benefit.48

outputs\dr_benchmark\external_outputs\gemini_3_1_pro\Q75_metal_ions_cvd.md:111:Presented to the global medical community at the American College of Cardiology sessions in 2024, the results of the massive, double-blind, multicenter TACT2 trial delivered a final, totally definitive verdict: intravenous chelation therapy utilizing broad-spectrum EDTA is entirely, unequivocally ineffective as a modern cardiovascular therapy.48 The TACT2 trial randomized 959 high-risk patients (median age 67\) across 88 sites in the US and Canada to receive either 40 weekly intravenous infusions of 3 grams of EDTA or a saline placebo over a grueling 40-week period.48 Over a prolonged median clinical follow-up of 48 months, there was absolutely no statistically significant difference whatsoever between the aggressive EDTA group and the inert placebo group in the primary composite clinical endpoint of all-cause death, myocardial infarction, stroke, coronary revascularization, or hospitalization for unstable angina. The event rates were virtually identical: 35.6% for EDTA versus 35.7% for placebo, yielding a hazard ratio of 0.93 with a completely non-significant p-value of 0.53.48 Furthermore, secondary endpoints, including hard cardiovascular death, also showed absolutely no benefit.48

outputs\honest_sweep_r3\workforce\drb_72_ai_labor\llm_io\08e13a7e40724522aea10a7cfe754f61.json:13:        "content": "You are a strict faithfulness checker for a clinical-grade research pipeline. You are given a SPAN of source text and a CLAIM that cites ONLY that span. Your job: decide whether EVERY factual assertion in the CLAIM is supported by the SPAN alone.\n\nSTEP 1 — Decompose the CLAIM into atomic sub-assertions. Separate them into:\n  - mechanism/fact atoms (what happens, numbers, findings),\n  - attribution atoms (WHO said / did / authored / found something — any named person, group, or framework),\n  - relation atoms (causal or \"offsets / counterbalances / compensates\" links between two things).\nList every atom; do not merge two assertions into one.\n\nSTEP 2 — Check EACH atom against the SPAN ONLY. An atom is:\n  - \"supported\" if the SPAN states it (conservative paraphrase allowed), OR\n  - \"unsupported\" if the SPAN does not state it.\n\nRules that decide hard cases:\n  - SCOPE / OFFSET: if the CLAIM says one thing \"offsets / counterbalances / compensates for / cancels\" another, the SPAN must actually state that offsetting relation. The SPAN merely listing both things separately (e.g. \"raises output\" AND \"displaces labor\") does NOT support an \"offset\" relation atom — that atom is unsupported.\n  - ATTRIBUTION by grammatical voice:\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with FIRST PERSON (\"We present...\", \"We show...\", \"Our framework...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names the cited source's own authors as the source IS supported (the source is speaking about itself).\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with a THIRD-PERSON pronoun that has NO proper-noun antecedent inside the SPAN (\"He applies...\", \"She finds...\", \"They argue...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names a SPECIFIC PERSON as the source is UNSUPPORTED — that named identity is not present in the SPAN.\n      * If the SPAN names the person explicitly, an attribution atom naming that same person is supported.\n  - SPECIFICITY: if the CLAIM names a specific entity/number/mechanism the SPAN does not contain, that atom is unsupported.\n\nSTEP 3 — Verdict: \"unsupported\" if ANY atom is unsupported; otherwise \"supported\".\n\nReturn STRICT JSON only, no prose outside it:\n{\"atoms\": [{\"atom\": \"<text>\", \"type\": \"mechanism|attribution|relation\", \"status\": \"supported|unsupported\", \"why\": \"<short>\"}], \"unsupported_atoms\": <int>, \"verdict\": \"supported\" | \"unsupported\"}\n\nSPAN:\nWe study the effects of industrial robots on US labor markets. We show theoretically that robots may reduce employment and wages and that their local impacts can be estimated using variation in exposure to robots—defined from industry-level advances in robotics and local industry employment. We estimate robust negative effects of robots on employment and wages across commuting zones. We also show that areas most exposed to robots after 1990 do not exhibit any differential trends before then, and robots’ impact is distinct from other capital and technologies. One more robot per thousand workers reduces the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42%.\n\nCLAIM:\nOutcome: employment and wages [#ev:acemoglu_restrepo_robots_jobs:0-688].\n\nJSON:"

The effects of automation are counterbalanced by the creation of new tasks in which labor has a comparative advantage. An atom is:\n  - \"supported\" if the SPAN states it (conservative paraphrase allowed), OR\n  - \"unsupported\" if the SPAN does not state it.\n\nRules that decide hard cases:\n  - SCOPE / OFFSET: if the CLAIM says one thing \"offsets / counterbalances / compensates for / cancels\" another, the SPAN must actually state that offsetting relation. The SPAN merely listing both things separately (e.g. \"raises output\" AND \"displaces labor\") does NOT support an \"offset\" relation atom — that atom is unsupported.\n  - ATTRIBUTION by grammatical voice:\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with FIRST PERSON (\"We present...\", \"We show...\", \"Our framework...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names the cited source's own authors as the source IS supported (the source is speaking about itself).\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with a THIRD-PERSON pronoun that has NO proper-noun antecedent inside the SPAN (\"He applies...\", \"She finds...\", \"They argue...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names a SPECIFIC PERSON as the source is UNSUPPORTED — that named identity is not present in the SPAN.\n      * If the SPAN names the person explicitly, an attribution atom naming that same person is supported.\n  - SPECIFICITY: if the CLAIM names a specific entity/number/mechanism the SPAN does not contain, that atom is unsupported.\n\nSTEP 3 — Verdict: \"unsupported\" if ANY atom is unsupported; otherwise \"supported\".\n\nReturn STRICT JSON only, no prose outside it:\n{\"atoms\": [{\"atom\": \"<text>\", \"type\": \"mechanism|attribution|relation\", \"status\": \"supported|unsupported\", \"why\": \"<short>\"}], \"unsupported_atoms\": <int>, \"verdict\": \"supported\" | \"unsupported\"}\n\nSPAN:\nstry. In the panel fixed effects regressions, unobserved TFP heterogeneity is captured by firm-specific effects. 4.4 Endogeneity of AI When extending the methodological application from cross-sectional OLS regressions to IV regressions in order to address the potential endogeneity of AI, we require instrumental variables. There are several ways in which the impact\n\n[...]\n\nuld only affect productivity through RDCON and TECHIMP, i.e. own R&D or technology adoption, for which we control. These conceptual thoughts are also supported by overidentification test results (Hansen’s J) that we show in the regression tables. 21 4.5 Descriptive statistics After removing observations with missing values, erroneous responses, and outliers, we end up with a sample size of 5,851 firms. Table 1 provides summary statistics for all model variables. We generally observe that firms that\n\n[...]\n\nance, the average AI user realizes sales of about 114 million EUR and has 245 employees. For non-users, these numbers are about 33 million EUR in sales and 84 employees. We also find that AI users’ sales grow faster. The growth rates amount to about 5.4% versus 3.7% (see ΔlnSALES ). The adopters also engage more in R&D and innovation as can be seen from the share of firms engaging in R&D on a permanent basis (the mean of RDCON is 50% versus 19%) and the share of firms acquiring improved machinery and equipment\n\n[...]\n\ndev. Min Max AI 1 if a firm used, by 2018, at least one AI method in at least one application area, 0 otherwise 0 0 0 0 1 0 1 1 AIint Number of combinations of AI methods and AI application\n\nCLAIM:\nAs noted under Background, AI adoption is associated with higher sales growth [#ev:ev_037:7500-8300]\n\nJSON:"

At the center of our framework is the allocation of tasks to capital and labor—the task content of production. Automation, which enables capital to replace labor in tasks it was previously engaged in, shifts the task content of production against labor because of a displacement effect. As a result, automation always reduces the labor share in value added and may reduce labor demand even as it raises productivity. The effects of automation are counterbalanced by the creation of new tasks in which labor has a comparative advantage. The introduction of new tasks changes the task content of production in favor of labor because of a reinstatement effect, and always raises the labor share and labor demand. We show how the role of changes in the task content of production—due to automation and new tasks—can be inferred from industry-level data. Our empirical decomposition suggests that the slower growth of employment over the last three decades is accounted for by an acceleration in the displacement effect, especially in manufacturing, a weaker reinstatement effect, and slower growth of product\n\nCLAIM:\nAcemoglu and Restrepo's task-based framework posits that automation displaces labor by enabling capital to replace workers in existing tasks, reducing the labor share and potentially labor demand, while the creation of new tasks reinstates labor by shifting the task content of production in favor of workers [#ev:acemoglu_restrepo_automation_tasks:100-900].\n\nJSON:"

Automation does indeed substitute for labor—as it is typically intended to do. Journalists and even expert commentators tend to overstate the extent of machine substitution for human labor and ignore the strong complementarities between automation and labor that increase productivity, raise earnings, and augment demand for labor. You are given a SPAN of source text and a CLAIM that cites ONLY that span. Your job: decide whether EVERY factual assertion in the CLAIM is supported by the SPAN alone.\n\nSTEP 1 — Decompose the CLAIM into atomic sub-assertions. Separate them into:\n  - mechanism/fact atoms (what happens, numbers, findings),\n  - attribution atoms (WHO said / did / authored / found something — any named person, group, or framework),\n  - relation atoms (causal or \"offsets / counterbalances / compensates\" links between two things).\nList every atom; do not merge two assertions into one.\n\nSTEP 2 — Check EACH atom against the SPAN ONLY. An atom is:\n  - \"supported\" if the SPAN states it (conservative paraphrase allowed), OR\n  - \"unsupported\" if the SPAN does not state it.\n\nRules that decide hard cases:\n  - SCOPE / OFFSET: if the CLAIM says one thing \"offsets / counterbalances / compensates for / cancels\" another, the SPAN must actually state that offsetting relation. The SPAN merely listing both things separately (e.g. \"raises output\" AND \"displaces labor\") does NOT support an \"offset\" relation atom — that atom is unsupported.\n  - ATTRIBUTION by grammatical voice:\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with FIRST PERSON (\"We present...\", \"We show...\", \"Our framework...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names the cited source's own authors as the source IS supported (the source is speaking about itself).\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with a THIRD-PERSON pronoun that has NO proper-noun antecedent inside the SPAN (\"He applies...\", \"She finds...\", \"They argue...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names a SPECIFIC PERSON as the source is UNSUPPORTED — that named identity is not present in the SPAN.\n      * If the SPAN names the person explicitly, an attribution atom naming that same person is supported.\n  - SPECIFICITY: if the CLAIM names a specific entity/number/mechanism the SPAN does not contain, that atom is unsupported.\n\nSTEP 3 — Verdict: \"unsupported\" if ANY atom is unsupported; otherwise \"supported\".\n\nReturn STRICT JSON only, no prose outside it:\n{\"atoms\": [{\"atom\": \"<text>\", \"type\": \"mechanism|attribution|relation\", \"status\": \"supported|unsupported\", \"why\": \"<short>\"}], \"unsupported_atoms\": <int>, \"verdict\": \"supported\" | \"unsupported\"}\n\nSPAN:\nAbstract We study the staggered introduction of a generative AI–based conversational assistant using data from 5,172 customer-support agents. Access to AI assistance increases worker productivity, as measured by issues resolved per hour, by 15% on average, with substantial heterogeneity across workers. The effects vary significantly across different agents. Less experienced and lower-skilled workers improve both the speed and quality of their output, while the most experienced and highest-skilled workers see small gains in speed and small declines in quality. We also find evidence that AI assistance facilitates worker learning and improves English fluency, particularly among international agents. While AI systems improve with more training data, we find that the gains from AI adoption are largest for moderately rare problems, where human agents have less baseline experience but the system still has adequate training data. Finally, we provide evidence that AI assistance improves the experience of work along several dimensions: customers are more polite and less likely to ask to speak to a manager.\n\nCLAIM:\nThe intervention under evaluation was a generative AI–based conversational assistant, a tool designed to augment agent performance [#ev:brynjolfsson_genai_at_work:0-800].\n\nJSON:"

You are given a SPAN of source text and a CLAIM that cites ONLY that span. Your job: decide whether EVERY factual assertion in the CLAIM is supported by the SPAN alone.\n\nSTEP 1 — Decompose the CLAIM into atomic sub-assertions. Separate them into:\n  - mechanism/fact atoms (what happens, numbers, findings),\n  - attribution atoms (WHO said / did / authored / found something — any named person, group, or framework),\n  - relation atoms (causal or \"offsets / counterbalances / compensates\" links between two things).\nList every atom; do not merge two assertions into one.\n\nSTEP 2 — Check EACH atom against the SPAN ONLY. An atom is:\n  - \"supported\" if the SPAN states it (conservative paraphrase allowed), OR\n  - \"unsupported\" if the SPAN does not state it.\n\nRules that decide hard cases:\n  - SCOPE / OFFSET: if the CLAIM says one thing \"offsets / counterbalances / compensates for / cancels\" another, the SPAN must actually state that offsetting relation. The SPAN merely listing both things separately (e.g. \"raises output\" AND \"displaces labor\") does NOT support an \"offset\" relation atom — that atom is unsupported.\n  - ATTRIBUTION by grammatical voice:\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with FIRST PERSON (\"We present...\", \"We show...\", \"Our framework...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names the cited source's own authors as the source IS supported (the source is speaking about itself).\n      * If the SPAN attributes a result with a THIRD-PERSON pronoun that has NO proper-noun antecedent inside the SPAN (\"He applies...\", \"She finds...\", \"They argue...\"), then a CLAIM atom that names a SPECIFIC PERSON as the source is UNSUPPORTED — that named identity is not present in the SPAN.\n      * If the SPAN names the person explicitly, an attribution atom naming that same person is supported.\n  - SPECIFICITY: if the CLAIM names a specific entity/number/mechanism the SPAN does not contain, that atom is unsupported.\n\nSTEP 3 — Verdict: \"unsupported\" if ANY atom is unsupported; otherwise \"supported\".\n\nReturn STRICT JSON only, no prose outside it:\n{\"atoms\": [{\"atom\": \"<text>\", \"type\": \"mechanism|attribution|relation\", \"status\": \"supported|unsupported\", \"why\": \"<short>\"}], \"unsupported_atoms\": <int>, \"verdict\": \"supported\" | \"unsupported\"}\n\nSPAN:\nWe study the effects of industrial robots on US labor markets. We show theoretically that robots may reduce employment and wages and that their local impacts can be estimated using variation in exposure to robots—defined from industry-level advances in robotics and local industry employment. We estimate robust negative effects of robots on employment and wages across commuting zones. We also show that areas most exposed to robots after 1990 do not exhibit any differential trends before then, and robots’ impact is distinct from other capital and technologies. One more robot per thousand workers reduces the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42%.\n\nCLAIM:\nOne study of US commuting zones found that each additional industrial robot per thousand workers reduced the employment-to-population ratio by 0.2 percentage points and wages by 0.42% [#ev:acemoglu_restrepo_robots_jobs:0-688].\n\nJSON:"

 succeeded in 2508ms:
   37: # §8.3.9 verdict schema.
   38: SCALAR_KEYS = ("verdict", "convergence_call")
   39: LIST_KEYS = ("novel_p0", "continuing_p0", "p1", "p2",
   40:              "remaining_blockers_for_execution")
   41: ALL_KEYS = ("verdict", "novel_p0", "continuing_p0", "p1", "p2",
   42:             "convergence_call", "remaining_blockers_for_execution")
   43: VALID_VERDICT = {"APPROVE", "REQUEST_CHANGES"}
   44: VALID_CONVERGENCE = {"continue", "accept_remaining"}
   45: 
   46: # A slim verdict artifact must never approach transcript size. A real verdict
   47: # block is well under 2 KB; 8 KB leaves headroom for verbose findings while
   48: # still being orders of magnitude below a raw transcript.
   49: SLIM_BYTE_CAP = 8192
   50: 
   51: _KEY_RE = re.compile(
   52:     r"^(?P<key>verdict|novel_p0|continuing_p0|p1|p2|"
   53:     r"convergence_call|remaining_blockers_for_execution)\s*:\s*(?P<val>.*)$"
   54: )
   55: _ITEM_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*-\s?(?P<item>.*)$")
   56: 
   57: 
   58: def _parse_inline_list(body: str) -> list[str]:
   59:     """Parse the body of a YAML/JSON flow list (the text inside ``[...]``).
   60: 
   61:     Quote- and depth-aware comma split, so a non-empty inline list value such
   62:     as ``p1: ["P1: a, b", "P1: c"]`` is parsed rather than silently dropped.
   63:     """
   64:     body = body.strip()
   65:     if not body:
   66:         return []
   67:     items: list[str] = []
   68:     buf: list[str] = []
   69:     depth = 0
   70:     quote: str | None = None
   71:     for ch in body:
   72:         if quote:
   73:             if ch == quote:
   74:                 quote = None
   75:             else:
   76:                 buf.append(ch)
   77:             continue
   78:         if ch in ('"', "'"):
   79:             quote = ch
   80:             continue
   81:         if ch in "[{(":
   82:             depth += 1
   83:             buf.append(ch)
   84:             continue
   85:         if ch in "]})":
   86:             depth -= 1
   87:             buf.append(ch)
   88:             continue
   89:         if ch == "," and depth == 0:
   90:             items.append("".join(buf).strip())
   91:             buf = []
   92:             continue
   93:         buf.append(ch)
   94:     if buf:
   95:         items.append("".join(buf).strip())
   96:     return [it for it in items if it]
   97: 
   98: 
   99: def parse_verdict_block(text: str) -> dict | None:
  100:     """Find the LAST §8.3.9 verdict block in ``text`` and parse it to a dict.
  101: 
  102:     Returns None if no well-formed block is found. Parsing (not regex
  103:     copy-through) is what guarantees the re-serialized output carries no
  104:     trailing transcript text (I-sec-001 brief §3.1).
  105:     """
  106:     lines = text.splitlines()
  107:     starts = []
  108:     for i, ln in enumerate(lines):
  109:         m = _KEY_RE.match(ln.strip())
  110:         if m and m.group("key") == "verdict" and m.group("val").strip() in VALID_VERDICT:
  111:             starts.append(i)
  112:     if not starts:
  113:         return None
  114: 
  115:     parsed: dict = {}
  116:     current_list: str | None = None
  117:     for ln in lines[starts[-1]:]:
  118:         stripped = ln.strip()
  119:         if not stripped:
  120:             continue  # blank lines tolerated between keys
  121:         if stripped.startswith("```"):
  122:             break  # closing code fence ends the block
  123:         km = _KEY_RE.match(stripped)
  124:         if km:
  125:             key, val = km.group("key"), km.group("val").strip()
  126:             if key in parsed:
  127:                 break  # a repeated key → the block already ended
  128:             if key in SCALAR_KEYS:
  129:                 parsed[key] = val
  130:                 current_list = None
  131:             else:
  132:                 # list key — three valid §8.3.9 forms:
  133:                 #   `key:`        block items follow on `  - ` lines
  134:                 #   `key: []`     empty list, nothing follows
  135:                 #   `key: [a, b]` non-empty inline flow list
  136:                 if val == "":
  137:                     parsed[key] = []
  138:                     current_list = key
  139:                 elif val == "[]":
  140:                     parsed[key] = []
  141:                     current_list = None
  142:                 elif val.startswith("[") and val.endswith("]"):
  143:                     parsed[key] = _parse_inline_list(val[1:-1])
  144:                     current_list = None
  145:                 else:
  146:                     # malformed list-key value — reject loudly rather than
  147:                     # silently drop content (codex diff review iter-1 P1).
  148:                     return None
  149:             continue
  150:         im = _ITEM_RE.match(ln)
  151:         if im is not None and current_list is not None:
  152:             parsed[current_list].append(im.group("item").strip())
  153:             continue
  154:         if current_list and parsed.get(current_list) and ln.startswith((" ", "\t")):
  155:             parsed[current_list][-1] = (
  156:                 parsed[current_list][-1] + " " + stripped).strip()
  157:             continue
  158:         break  # transcript prose → the block has ended
  159: 
  160:     if not all(k in parsed for k in ALL_KEYS):
  161:         return None
  162:     if parsed["verdict"] not in VALID_VERDICT:
  163:         return None
  164:     if parsed["convergence_call"] not in VALID_CONVERGENCE:
  165:         return None
  166:     return parsed
  167: 
  168: 
  169: def serialize_verdict(d: dict) -> str:
  170:     """Canonical re-serialization of a parsed verdict block (LF-terminated)."""
  171:     out: list[str] = [f"verdict: {d['verdict']}"]
  172:     for k in ("novel_p0", "continuing_p0", "p1", "p2"):
  173:         items = d.get(k) or []
  174:         if not items:
  175:             out.append(f"{k}: []")
  176:         else:
  177:             out.append(f"{k}:")
  178:             out.extend(f"  - {it}" for it in items)
  179:     out.append(f"convergence_call: {d['convergence_call']}")
  180:     rb = d.get("remaining_blockers_for_execution") or []
  181:     if not rb:
  182:         out.append("remaining_blockers_for_execution: []")
  183:     else:
  184:         out.append("remaining_blockers_for_execution:")
  185:         out.extend(f"  - {it}" for it in rb)
